coredata.dc:title: Copper sulfide (CuS) doped with carbon quantum dots (CQD) as an efficient photo catalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Iqbal, Tahir
Ashraf, Maria
Afsheen, Sumera
Masood, Arslan
Qureshi, Muhammad Tauseef
Obediat, Sofian Talal
Hamed, Mahmoud Fawzy
Othman, Mohamed S.
coredata.dc:description: In environmental pollutant remediation, advanced photocatalyst is essential for waste water purification. A facile and cost-effective technique was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQD) which were utilized to modify copper sulfide (CuS). The 2% and 4% (CuS-CQDs) has been prepared by hydrothermal method. CuS-CQD has numerous photo-generated charge transfers as compared to pure CuS attributed to the synergistic effect of CuS, with CQDs. Prepared materials were utilized to photo-catalytic activity to breakdown Panadol effect from waste water. The 4% CuS-CQD exhibits a lower band gap energy and higher transient photocurrent density as compare to pure CuS and 2% CuS-CQD, as examined by optical and photoluminescence spectrum. Under visible-light irradiation, the 4% CuS-CQD exhibits much higher photocatalytic activity and stability towards the degradation of Panadol than other synthesized materials. On the basis of the experimental results and literature reports, a plausible visible-light driven photocatalytic mechanism of CuS-CQD for degrading Panadol was also proposed. In this research work, it has been reported that 4% CuS-CQD exhibited exceptional (96.5%) photo-degradation performance against Panadol. Carbon quantum dotes helped to increase the separation of photo generated charge carriers, allowing CuS to stimulate pollutant degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper sulfide (CuS)
Carbon quantum dots (CQD)
Panadol powder
Photocatalysis
Hydrothermal method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CuS depend with CQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of CuS with Sodium thiosulfate and copper sulphate using hydrothermal process.
Synthesis process of carbon quantum dots through waste peanut shells and concentrated H2SO4.
Production of CuS doped with CQD by hydrothermal process.
Photocatalytic performance of CuS dopend CQDs.
SEM micrograph of (a) pure CuS, (b), 1% CuS doped CQD (c) 2% CuS doped CQD and (d) 4% CuS doped CQD.
(a) Micrographs of the Copper sulfide, (b) carbon quantum dots, (c & d) 2% and 4% CQDs doped samples respectively.
Micrograph of the XRD results of the CuS, CQDs, 2% and 4% CuS-CQDs samples.

(a) Photocurrent response of pure and doped CuS in phosphate buffered solution depicting the significant reduction in electron hole recombination rate, (b) PL Emission spectrum of synthesized samples.
(a) FTIR of the synthesis CQDs, (b) FTIR spectrum of prepared materials.
Raman Spectrum of the prepared materials CuS, 2% CQDs, and 4% CQDs sequentially.
(a) the effect of adsorption on photocatalytic degradation of pollutant, (b) logarithmic graph of Degradation spectrums of prepared samples for the removal of panadol.
(left) Reusability graph, (right) Depicting the effect of radical scavenger for 4% CuS-CQDs sample.
XRD spectrum of 4% CQDs-CuS before and after five cycles depicting reduction in the crystallinity.
Physical properties of the prepared material.

head.ce:title: Copper sulfide (CuS) doped with carbon quantum dots (CQD) as an efficient photo catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Gujrat, Hafiz Hayat Campus, Gujrat, 50700, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Gujrat, Hafiz Hayat Campus, Gujrat, 50700, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, Faculty of Science, University of Gujrat, Hafiz Hayat Campus, Gujrat, 50700, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Zoology, Faculty of Science, University of Gujrat, Hafiz Hayat Campus, Gujrat, 50700, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Sciences, College of Preparatory Year, University of Ha'il, Ha'il, 2440, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Basic Sciences, College of Preparatory Year, University of Ha’il, Ha’il 2440, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In environmental pollutant remediation, advanced photocatalyst is essential for waste water purification. A facile and cost-effective technique was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQD) which were utilized to modify copper sulfide (CuS). The 2% and 4% (CuS-CQDs) has been prepared by hydrothermal method. CuS-CQD has numerous photo-generated charge transfers as compared to pure CuS attributed to the synergistic effect of CuS, with CQDs. Prepared materials were utilized to photo-catalytic activity to breakdown Panadol effect from waste water. The 4% CuS-CQD exhibits a lower band gap energy and higher transient photocurrent density as compare to pure CuS and 2% CuS-CQD, as examined by optical and photoluminescence spectrum. Under visible-light irradiation, the 4% CuS-CQD exhibits much higher photocatalytic activity and stability towards the degradation of Panadol than other synthesized materials. On the basis of the experimental results and literature reports, a plausible visible-light driven photocatalytic mechanism of CuS-CQD for degrading Panadol was also proposed. In this research work, it has been reported that 4% CuS-CQD exhibited exceptional (96.5%) photo-degradation performance against Panadol. Carbon quantum dotes helped to increase the separation of photo generated charge carriers, allowing CuS to stimulate pollutant degradation.

Production of CuS dopend with CQD by hydrothermal process.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The growth of human population has resulted in a slew of environmental and pollution issues. Among all of these issues, water contamination is the most harmful to human life. Water is a crucial part for life on this planet, it has direct impact on all living things. According to global survey results, about half of the world's population does not have access to fresh and clean water for drinking. As a result, the first concern of every human being on the earth is to protect water from all forms of pollution. Many contaminants, such as dyes, heavy metals, pharmaceutical, paper, organic & inorganic materials have been found in our drinking water, posing major health risks. Pharmaceuticals are a sort of water pollution that is becoming more widespread as a result of its long-term hazardous effects on humans and animals [1].

Panadol is a pharmaceutical product that is also classified as an organic contaminant. It is also one among the top three most commonly prescribed drugs in the world. Because of its negative effects on the aquatic environment, humans, and animals. The presence of Panadol in the aquatic environment and water has caused alarming situation. Its content in water has risen above the permissible limit, posing a serious threat to human health and aquatic life. Phenol is used as a chemical reagent in the commercial production of Panadol. Phenol has also been regarded as a hazardous and dangerous substance [2].

The international community and researchers in the field have made significant contributions and efforts to reduce the hazardous effects of Panadol in the modern era. Due to its long-lasting effects, full Panadol breakdown is difficult. However, by employing environmentally friendly techniques, these effects can be mitigated and converted into something less destructive [3]. Radiolysis, ozonolysis, sonolysis, photo-femto reaction, and photocatalysis have all been used to eliminate organic contaminants from waste water. Photocatalysis was chosen as a method for Panadol degradation in this study because it is a renewable, cost-effective and most suited method. The photocatalysis is an effective method because there has been a lot of research done. There are bundles of volumes in the literature to improve the photocatalysis process for the degradation of water contaminants. The fundamental benefit of photocatalysis is that it is a sustainable process [4].

CuS has recently become popular in photocatalytic applications due to its high theoretical capacity (561 mA h g−1), metal-like electronic conductivity, and intricate structure. Due to their exceptional optical characteristics, metal sulphide semiconductors have a lot of potential in photocatalysts. Controlling the shape and particle size of metal sulphide semiconductors can change the optical band gap without changing the chemical composition of the material. Copper sulphide, like other metal sulphide compounds, exhibits amazing properties. Use of CuS has been beneficial for the removal of pharmaceutical products. Carbon quantum dots provide large surface area for the absorption of contaminants from waste water. Carbon quantum dots have high porosity, large surface area, and excellent electron transfer capacity, relatively low cost and has high aqueous stability. Due to the high porosity of carbon, it has been used as an absorbent to absorb impurities from water [5].

Theerthagiri, J., et al., synthesis CuS-WO3 composites to analyze the performance of photocatalytic activities. They utilized the hydrothermal method for its preparation. Prepared samples varied the energy band-gap from 2.25 eV to 1.87 eV which was a good approach in band-gap reduction but the degradation efficiency of methylene blue was 75% reported [6].

Shahzad, Khurram, et al. manipulated Nano plates of AC/WO3 for the improvement of photocatalytic applications. They prepared activated carbon from sugarcane brags and tungsten trioxide. They used the hydrothermal method for the preparation of composites. Their results were dynamic nano-plates effectively increased the degradation rate and doping of activated carbon in WO3 reduced the bandgap of the Copper sulfide and its doped with carbon quantum dots have been prepared which are high efficient and cost-effective than reported. CuS-CQD 2% and 4% was prepared by hydrothermal method for the degradation of panadol drug from waste water. It has been notice that 4% CuS-CQD is the materials. But the efficiency level was not high for pharmaceutical materials [7].

Above discussed literature review shows that their results were less efficient for the removal of drugs from wastewater. In this research work most suited and efficient sample (prepared by hydrothermal method) for the decomposition of organic contaminants to get the beneficial results. 2% CuS-CQD shows the 87.9% efficiency for the degradation of panadol but 4% CuS-CQD reveal 96.5% removal of drug from water in 120 min. So prepared 4% CuS-CQD catalyst is more convenient, unique and energy saving as compared to above other catalysts and has greater efficiency for the degradation of panadol.

In this research study, the following chemicals were used: The hydrothermal approach was utilized to synthesize Copper sulphide utilizing 50 g sugarcane barges powder, 20 ml sulfuric acid (H2SO4), 10 ml hydrochloric acid (HCL), 100 ml distilled water, 5 ml ethanol (C2H5OH), 1 g Panadol powder, Copper sulphate pent hydrate (CuSO4•5H2O), sodium thiosulfate pen 200 mL deionized water, 50 mL isopropanol, CTAB (C19H42BrN) cetrimonium bromide [8].

To make copper sulphide, dissolve 2 g sodium thiosulfate in 100 ml deionized water and stir for 20 min at room temperature to produce a serum-like solution with thiosulfate ions (S2O3 2−), which function as a reducing agent. 2 g copper sulphate was added to the generated serum while stirring, resulting in cupric ions (Cu2+) in the solution, which functioned as an oxidizer and caused a redox reaction in the compounds. Copper sulphate became colorless and eventually black when reacted with sodium thiosulfate serum [8]. 0.05 g of Cetrimonium bromide (CTAB) was added to the solution as a surfactant to reduce the surface tension of the components. CuS was prepared by utilizing the hydrothermal method, which involved placing a solution in an autoclave at 150 °C for 6 h. The sample was then allowed to cool down at ambient temperature. The synthesis sample was centrifuged and dried in a Muffled oven at 80 °C. The production of pure dry black copper sulphide is depicted in Fig. 1 .

Waste peanut shell was collected from a local market for the manufacture of Carbon Quantum dots. The peanut shells were rinsed with tap water first, then deionized water three to four times. The peanut shells then were left out in the sun to dry entirely. A tiny grinder machine was used to process these dried peanut shells into powder. To complete the chemical reaction, around 25 ml of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was added to the powder, which weighed about 250 g, in the exhaust chamber. After that, it was saturated, yielding a homogenous mixture [9].

Sulfuric acid-absorbent material was dried for 4 h at 250 °C in a Muffled oven. Pestle mortar was used to ground the prepared sample. Wash the sample 5–6 times with deionized water until the pH reaches 7, and then dry it for 2 h at 100 °C in the microwave oven. As a result of this technique, a fine powder form of carbon quantum dots was formed from the dried material. Fig. 2
illustrates the complete method of preparation [10] (see Fig. 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CuS depend with CQD
ce:section.ce:para: To make cupper sulfide-based CQD samples, dissolve 1 g of cupper sulphide in 100 mL of deionized water, and added 0.02 g of CQD powder. The solution was spiked with 0.01 g of cetrimonium bromide CTAB, which lowers surface tension and reduces quantum dot size. In the darkroom, the solution was constantly swirled at 300 rpm. The sample was then rinsed with ethanol using a centrifuge machine and dried in a muffle furnace at 80 °C for 2 h after being placed in the autoclave at 150 °C temperatures for 6 h. As a result, a dried form of 2% CuS dependent CQD was prepared however, for 4% CuS dependent CQD, the same technique was used, but only 0.04 g of CQD was taken [11].

Using a homemade reactor, the photocatalytic properties of all generated compounds were assessed. The photocatalytic characteristics of all manufactured samples were evaluated after irradiating under visible light of 400 W mercury lamp for this purpose. 2 mg panadol was dissolved in 100 mL water in a beaker and thoroughly stirred for this purpose, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a-f) . The material was then dissolved in panadol aqueous solution beakers at a concentration of 20 mg. For 30 min, the solution was agitated without light to reach adsorption and desorption equilibrium (Fig. 11(a)). Using UV–Vis spectroscopy, a few ml sample solutions were removed at equal intervals (30 min) to check for deterioration. Fig. 4 (e, d) clearly demonstrated the degradation of panadol. It has been observed that a significant amount of drug contamination is degraded after 150 min.

The obtained samples evaluated for structural, morphological, vibrational, optical, and photocatalytic properties. The “Bruker D8 X-ray Diffraction technique with Cu-k x-ray source = (1.541874)" was used to collect structural characteristics in the 2 range of 25–75° with a 20/min step. The morphology of the manufactured samples was determined using “TESCAN MAIA3 Field emission Scanning Electron Microscopy.” The existence of functional groups in materials was discovered using Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR-4100 type-A, JASCO) spectroscopy [12]. Using a 320 nm excitation wavelength, photoluminescence spectroscopy (FP-8200 Spectrofluorometer JASCO) yielded emission spectra in the range of 320 nm–520 nm. Finally, photocatalytic activity was measured using V-730 Ultraviolet-Visible Spectroscopy [13].

With the use of an analytic micrograph, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) is used to analyze the morphology of the prepared materials. Fig. 5 shows a surface micrograph of pure copper sulphide and carbon quantum dots doped samples. Cupper sulphide is shown to have a nano-flower-like structure, shape, and size, length, and diameter that changes when the CQD concentration ratio changes. In a SEM micrograph of pure copper sulphide (CuS), a clear flower-like structure can be seen at 500 nm, with flower petals ranging in size from 60 to 69 nm [3]. Carbon possesses a microspores structure with a huge surface area, which has been effective in improving CuS characteristics efficiency. Fig. 5 (b, c), and (d) show 2%, and 4% samples, respectively.

It was discovered that as the amount of carbon in the atmosphere grows, the nano-flowers gradually transform. Because of the doping of CQDs, the morphology has become more acute and distinct. Doping increases the surface area of the copper sulphide, as shown in a micrograph of a CQDs-doped sample. The production electron-hole recombination rate is affected by this change in shape, as well as the bandgap of the manufactured samples. The inclusion of CQDs causes nano-flowers to sprout in all directions. By altering the temperature and time of the material, the hydrothermal process gives a control morphological facility [14].

The sharpness of the nano-flowers is directly related to the %age of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 (c, d). The size of the rods is also shown to be in the range of 50 nm–58 nm, which is smaller than the size of pure CuS. The efficacy of the developed catalyst for photocatalytic degradation is improved by reducing the size of the materials. It has been discovered that produced samples contain small nano-flowers with nano-petals, which increase the sample's absorbance area, which is beneficial to panadol breakdown [15].

Using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, the composition of the synthesis peanut shells base carbon quantum dots, cupper sulphides, and their doped samples was determined (EDX). The results of the EDX spectrum were observed and are shown in fig (6) . The EDX data clearly indicates the presence of a considerable amount of carbon, with a small amount of oxygen, sulphur, and calcium.

The presence of oxygen, calcium, and sulphur was an impurity in the element's constitution. The presence of sulphur atoms caused pores to form on the surface of the material, which was caused by the sulfuric acid (H2SO4) employed in the carbon preparation procedure. Ionized water was used to wash the samples so that oxygen could remain in it. From the calculations, it was determined that the prepared CuS sample includes 61.63% Copper, 38.37% sulphur and Fig. 6 (b) is the EXD spectra of CQDs which contain 79.95% carbon, 3.89% sulphur, 9.29% oxygen and 6.87% calcium in K and L shells [16].

An impurity in the element's makeup was the presence of oxygen, calcium, and sulphur. The sulfuric acid (H2SO4) used in the carbon preparation technique caused pores to form on the material's surface because of the presence of sulphur atoms. The samples were washed with ionized water to keep the oxygen in them. The calculated CuS sample contains 61.63% copper, 38.37% sulphur, and Fig. 6 (b) shows the EXD spectra of CQDs in K and L shells, which include 79.95% carbon, 3.89% sulphur, 9.29% oxygen, and 6.87% calcium.

Powder XRD was used to investigate the phase structure and content of the synthesis samples. Fig shows the structures of pure CuS, CQDs, 2% CuS-CQD, and 4% CuS-CQD. With JCPDS data, pure covellite CuS exhibits hexagonal phase, as can be seen in the patterns. The cell parameters are a = 3.792 A and c = 16.344 A, and all diffraction peaks were tightly indexed with the standard peaks of CuS hexagonal phase with JCPDS card number 06–0464. There was no further impurity peaks observed. The diffraction peaks are substantially wider than the typical peaks, indicating the creation of smaller crystallites [17]. The XRD of CQDs is shown in Fig. 7 with the @ symbol, the spectrum shows the indicated peak at (002) which conform the purity of the CQDs. The peaks for 4% CuS-CQD are stronger and sharper, as seen in Fig. 7, implying that the as-obtained products are well crystalline and smaller in size. This could be attributed to a higher concentration of CQD until a well-defined stable structure is achieved. There is the no extra phase was generated due to the addition of CQDs only the peaks of the sample is slightly moves downward. According to the JCPDS file (06–0464), the XRD graph clearly indicates peak at (100), (101), (102), (103), (008), (110), (108), and (116), showing that CuS exists as a covellite phase. These peaks are consistent with prior studies. Using Scherrer's formula, the crystalline size of CuS, 2% CuS-CQD, and 4% CuS-CQD nanomaterials was calculated:
d = k λ / β
cos
θ
Where “d" represents the mean crystalline dimension, “k" represents the crystallite form constant (0.9),” represents the X-ray wavelength (Cu K: 1.5406A)" represents the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the peak in radians, and "*" represents the slight change in the spectrum of the prepared samples. CuS, 2% CuS-CQD, and 4% CuS-CQD had typical grain sizes of 20, 18, and 13 nm, respectively.

The average grain size was found to be 20, 18, and 13 nm for CuS, 2% CuS-CQD and 4% CuS-CQD, respectively. The computed surface area and diameter of the microcrystalline synthesis sample are shown in Table 1 . The ultrafine particles in the created material contribute to the porosity structure.

PL spectroscopy was used to investigate the optical properties of CuS, CQDs and its CQDs doped CuS samples, including the recombination rate of photo-generated electron/hole pairs. Fig. 8 depicts the spectra of all samples. The PL spectroscopy was performed at a wavelength of 350 nm. The spectra demonstrate that copper sulphide emits at 490 nm, while the 2% CuS-CQD sample has a light wavelength variation and reduces the peak intensity of the copper sulphide. Carbon Quantum dots has very small intensity at that excitation wavelength due to its low electronegativity which about 2.55. It is an organic material when it react with CuS its reduces electron hole recombination rate. The sample includes 4% CuS-CQD and has a wavelength of 491.2 nm, indicating that doping of carbon quantum dots lowers the bandgap of the pure material and shifts the peak downward, lowering the electron-hole recombination rate, which is the best component for photocatalytic destruction of contaminants [18]. When 4% CQD is applied to the produced sample, the PL spectrum shows a dynamic change, with the intensity peak moving downward with a large gap and the wavelength of the sample increasing to 422 nm, causing a change in the bandgap of the material. The photocurrent responses of different electrodes were measured at 0 V with visible light stimulation in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing ascorbic acid (AA, 0.1 M). Fig. 8(a). Increase in photocurrent with dopant concentration is direct evidence of reduced electron hole recombination rate in the fabricated material.

CQD reduces the intensity of the prepared samples while increasing the wavelength, reducing the bandgap. The provided PL spectrum aids in the investigation of the prepared samples' original wavelength and intensity shifts. Peaks of PL are related to the response of photo-generated electron/hole pair recombination, and the lowest PL intensity of 4% CuS-CQD samples showed that recombination in this sample might be significantly slower during photocatalysis study [19].

The IR spectrum was used to explore the functional groups in terms of wavenumber (cm)−1, and the IR spectra of all the processed samples are given in Fig. 9 . This spectrum has also shown the synthesis materials' band vibrations, which are used to prepare proof samples. The y-axis represents the %age transition, while the x-axis represents the wavenumber (cm)−1.

The maximum adsorption rate was obtained at 3035.05 cm−1 because of bonded –OH groups, while the lowest adsorption rate was detected at 2109.06 cm−1 due to the vibrational frequency of CC stretching. The stretching vibration C–O–C provided the adsorption rate with the lowest value, 1156.61 cm−1. Where as in Fig. 9 (b) the band at 628 cm−1 confirm the presence of copper sulfide Cu–S and the band present in all samples at 804.1 cm−1 was due to C–O stretching vibration. The presence of Cu–S was also confirmed by the peaks at 1096 cm−1 wavenumber. Furthermore, the spectrum of 2% CQDs doped sample shows that the bend of the tungsten trioxide moves downward and 4% CQDs doped sample also conform this phenomenon that when %age doping of activated carbon increase then the bend 3025 and 1651 cm−1 increases [20]. The R–NCS bending edge detected at 2155.2 cm−1 confirms the presence of CTAB, which has been used to prepare the samples and lowers particle size, making them more refined and clear. 3506.1 cm−1 is the O–H band that was calculated using the hydrothermal method since samples are left in the water for 4 h at 150 °C, indicating the presence of the H–O–H group [21].

The atomic vibration pattern of a CuS and CQDs doped synthesis samples was studied using Raman spectroscopy. The surface morphology is calculated at 5 nm, which is the threshold limit of this approach, Raman spectroscopy may be employed in situ to identify surface species and spatial variations in composition. This technique is unique for gaining a deeper understanding of that complex system Fig. 10
shows the Raman spectra of CuS, 2% and 4% CQDs doped materials in the 0-2000 cm−1 wavelength region. The Raman spectra of CuS revealed a high peak at 965 cm−1, which corresponds to the A1g vibration mode of CuS. Raman spectra of 4% CuS-CQDs revealed peak and depth in cure at 965 cm−1 which induced D mode and in plane vibrational G mode of CQDs, the slight very small peaks less than 965 cm−1 has also been observed which was due to the 2D, D + G, and 2D′ Raman bands respectively. For the 4% CuS-CQDs composite, the typical Raman peaks of CuS and CQDs were found, which depicted the CuS-CQDs interaction. Fig. 10 reveal the addition of CQDs in CuS reduces the edges of the spectrum.

Photocatalytic activity has been performed four times, each for 150 min to understand the nature of the fabricated materials with Panadol solution. Through light degradation of Panadol medication as a pollutant, the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized CuS and its CQDs doped was examined. While performing photocatalytic activity, after adding photocatalyst to the contaminated water, 30 min stirring is done in the dark to obtain absorption-adsorption equilibrium. As shown in Fig. 11(a) Its effect is negligible as compared to catalytic activity. Moreover, the adsorption is increased with the increase in doping concentration of CQDs attributing to the fact that doping has changed the morphology and maybe provided more surface area due to reduced particle size The doping of CQDs in pure CuS sample is an excellent decision, as can be observed. When compared pure CuS samples with 4% CuS- CQDs doped sample, then it shows significant degradation rate. As indicated in Fig. 11, increasing the amount of CQDs in CuS significantly boosted the degrading efficiency of catalysts. After equal interval of about 30 min, 4 ml of sample was taken out of the produced solution to get UV–Vis spectra. The deterioration rate of the produced materials was investigated using the UV–vis spectrum. For simplicity, we have mentioned a graph between ln(C0/C) and time as shown in Fig. 11 (b).

The greatest efficiency was estimated for a 4% CuS-CQDs sample. The photo-catalysis process was improved because the bandgap had more trapping sites, which lowered the rate of electron/hole pair recombination, giving radicals more time to react with the pollutant. The following formula was used to investigate the efficiency of the manufactured materials using the kinetic theory of the degrading process.
Degradation % =
(
1 −
C
C o
)
× 100

The beginning concentration of the solution is Co in the following formula, and the ultimate concentration of the solution is C after time ‘t’. Panadol degradation was exceedingly low in the absence of a catalyst, even approaching nil. Panadol is an extremely stable contaminant that displays no signs of deterioration. When 0.1 mg of copper sulfide was added to that aqueous solution, however, after 150 min, around 76.9% of the drug was removed, as seen in Fig. 11.

Interestingly, the degradation efficiency of 2% CuS-CQDs was degraded 87.9%, while the degradation efficiency of 4% CuS-CQDs sample was calculated to be 96.5%. Furthermore, as demonstrated in Fig. 11, the effectiveness of concentration degradation is proportional to the exposure period. The addition of CQDs, which provides a high surface area for the absorption of organic contaminants from water, is linked to an increase in percentage degradation. As a result, the efficiency of charge separation of water molecules improves.

The decomposition efficiency of pure tungsten trioxide rose dramatically when the concentration ratio of CuS increased. When light with a threshold frequency falls on the prepared photocatalyst during photocatalytic activity, the valence band electrons are activated and migrate to the conduction band, while the same numbers of holes are created in the valence band. CuS-CQDs materials operate as a scavenger during this process, reducing the rate of electron and hole recombination. The following is a list of probable redox reactions.
CuS - CQD + h υ
→ CuS - CQD
(
e -
)
+ CuS - CQD
(
h +
)
CuS-CQD (h+) + drug → drug+• + CuS-CQD
CuS-CQD (h+) +H2O → CuS-CQD + OH− + H+
CuS-CQD (h+) + OH- → CuS-CQD + OH•
CuS-CQD (e⁻) + O2 → CuS-CQD + O2 •⁻
O2•⁻-OH• + (Panadol) Drug → CO2 + H2O

When a CuS catalyst reacts with a concentration of 4% CQDs combines with water, it produces hydroxyl radicals and hydrogen ions. When light strikes the produced catalyst, it causes holes to appear on its surface, as shown in Fig. 12 .

The purpose of the scavenger studies was to find the active species responsible for the photocatalytic breakdown of panadol. Fig. 13 shows the scavenger experiment results. Different quenching agents were utilized in these tests, including disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (Na2-EDTA), tert-butanol (TBA), and para benzoquinone (BZQ). The use of TBA significantly decreased degradation of panadol from 96% to 25%. Furthermore, the presence of BZQ has reduced the proportion of panadol removed to 38%. Surprisingly, the addition of Na2-EDTA results in just a slight decrease in degrading efficiency from 96 to 73%. The fact that O 2- and OH are the active radical species in the photocatalysis process accounts for the considerable decrease in catalytic degradation of panadol Drug in the presence of TBA and BZQ. Furthermore, the minor reduction in degradation efficiency in the presence of Na2-EDTA indicates that holes (h+) are not responsible for panadol degradation.

The photo-stability and reusability of 4% CuS-CQDs were determined, which is a crucial feature to consider when designing an effective catalyst. This was accomplished by calculating the sample's recycle rate. The sample was magnetically isolated for this reason, and aspirin degradation was repeated five times under the same photocatalytic conditions as before. The Photo-stability and Reusability data (%age) deterioration for four Cycles is shown in Fig. 13. The efficiency did not decline much after four cycles, with an efficiency of 96.5% compared to 90.1% after the first cycle. The XRD analysis of the sample after five cycles revealed that crystallinity of the sample has decreased (Fig. 14 ).

The hydrothermal approach was used to successfully synthesize CuS and it's doped samples with varied CQDs concentration ratios (2% and 4%). The structural, morphological, compositional, and optical properties of the produced samples were determined using FTIR, XRD, SEM, PL Spectroscopy and Raman Spectroscopy. The decomposition of Panadol under visible light irradiation in the photocatalytic reactor was used to test the materials' photocatalytic activity. The SEM images reveal nano-flower-shaped of various sizes and lengths. The PL results reveal that increasing the CuS content ratio with CQDs reduced crystalline and created flaws, as well as confirming the presence of CuS in the manufactured samples. The photocatalytic stability of all 4% CuS-CQDs composites is higher than that of pure CuS. The two materials composite found to be the most effective catalyst for pharmaceutical degradation. Due to improved morphology and reduced electron/hole recombination rate, it destroys about 96.5% of Panadol in 150 min. These elements are crucial in the breakdown of pollutants in wastewater.
“Not applicable”.
“Not applicable”.
“Not applicable”.

The author (s) greatly acknowledges the mutual cooperation/collaboration, facilitation and financial support/assistance by the Scientific Research Deanship at University of Ha'il-Saudi Arabia through project number RG-21 063.
“All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article”.

Tahir Iqbal: Conceptualization, and, Supervision. Maria Ashraf: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Sumera Afsheen: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Arslan Masood: Visualization, Software, Investigation. Muhammad Tauseef Qureshi: Resources, and, Funding acquisition. Sofian Talal Obediat: Funding acquisition, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mahmoud Fawzy Hamed: Funding acquisition, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed S. Othman: Funding acquisition, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
